turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're diving into AI privacy — a tension between machine learning's hunger for data and our right to control personal information. To kick us off, what's the core privacy challenge that concerns you most?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Training data memorization. These models don't just learn patterns—they can regurgitate specific personal details verbatim. A 2021 study showed GPT-2 could be coaxed into outputting real names, phone numbers, and addresses from its training data. That's not statistics; that's a privacy breach baked into the model itself.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's chilling — the idea that personal data is somehow embedded in the model's very fabric. So if someone realizes their information is in there, can they actually get it removed?

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That sounds like a legal right running straight into a technical wall. How does something like Europe's "right to be forgotten" actually work when you can't just surgically remove one person's data from a neural network?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: It doesn't work well. Neural networks encode information in a distributed, entangled way—think of it like trying to un-bake a cake to remove one specific egg. The data is woven throughout millions of parameters, making true deletion practically impossible without retraining the entire model from scratch.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So if neither legal rights nor technical fixes can easily extract personal data from these models

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So if neither legal rights nor technical fixes can easily extract personal data from these models, what does a realistic path forward look like? Where should we be focusing our hope?
